([French](/wiki/French_language "French language"): *Jeux olympiques d'été de 1924*), officially the **Games of the VIII Olympiad** ([French](/wiki/French_language "French language"): *Jeux de la VIIIe olympiade*) and officially branded as **Paris 1924**, were an international [multi-sport event](/wiki/Multi-sport_event "Multi-sport event") held in [Paris](/wiki/Paris "Paris"), France. The opening ceremony was held on 5 July, but some competitions had already started on 4 May. The Games were the second to be hosted by Paris (after [1900](/wiki/1900_Summer_Olympics "1900 Summer Olympics")), making it the first city to host the Olympics twice.

The **1924 Summer Olympics** ([French](/wiki/French_language "French language"): *Jeux olympiques d'été de 1924*), officially the **Games of the VIII Olympiad** ([French](/wiki/French_language "French language"): *Jeux de la VIIIe olympiade*) and officially branded as **Paris 1924**, were an international [multi-sport event](/wiki/Multi-sport_event "Multi-sport event") held in [Paris](/wiki/Paris "Paris"), France. The opening ceremony was held on 5 July, but some competitions had already started on 4 May. The Games were the second to be hosted by Paris (after [1900](/wiki/1900_Summer_Olympics "1900 Summer Olympics")), making it the first city to host the Olympics twice.

The selection process for the 1924 Summer Olympics consisted of six bids, and Paris was selected ahead of [Amsterdam](/wiki/Amsterdam "Amsterdam"), [Barcelona](/wiki/Barcelona "Barcelona"), [Los Angeles](/wiki/Los_Angeles "Los Angeles"), [Prague](/wiki/Prague "Prague"), and [Rome](/wiki/Rome "Rome"). The selection was made at the 20th IOC Session in [Lausanne](/wiki/Lausanne "Lausanne") in 1921.[[2]](#cite_note-votes-2) The cost of these Games was estimated to be 10,000,000 [F](/wiki/French_franc "French franc") (equivalent to [€](/wiki/Euro "Euro")10.1 million in 2022[[3]](#cite_note-inflation-FR-3)). With total receipts at 5,496,610 F (equivalent to €5.53 million in 2022), the Olympics resulted in a hefty loss despite daily crowds of up to 60,000.[[4]](#cite_note-Zarnowski-4) The United States won the most gold and overall medals, having 229 athletes competing compared to France's 401. This would be the last Summer Games hosted by France until 2024.

## Highlights

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1924_Summer_Olympics&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Highlights")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dc/Stade_de_Colombes_1924.jpg/250px-Stade_de_Colombes_1924.jpg)](/wiki/File:Stade_de_Colombes_1924.jpg)

Colombes Olympic Stadium

* The [Uruguay national football team](/wiki/Uruguay_national_football_team "Uruguay national football team") won the gold medal in football, which was recognized as a world championship by [FIFA](/wiki/FIFA "FIFA").
* The opening ceremony and several sporting events took place in the [Olympic Stadium](/wiki/Stade_Olympique_Yves-du-Manoir "Stade Olympique Yves-du-Manoir") of [Colombes](/wiki/Colombes "Colombes"), which had a capacity of 45,000 in 1924.
* This VIII Olympiad was the last one organized under the presidency of [Pierre de Coubertin](/wiki/Pierre_de_Coubertin "Pierre de Coubertin").
* The "[Flying Finns](/wiki/Flying_Finn "Flying Finn")" dominated the long-distance running, while the British and Americans dominated the shorter events. [Paavo Nurmi](/wiki/Paavo_Nurmi "Paavo Nurmi") won the 1500 m and 5,000 m (which were held with only an hour between them) and the cross-country run. [Ville Ritola](/wiki/Ville_Ritola "Ville Ritola") won the 10,000 m and the 3,000 m [steeplechase](/wiki/Steeplechase_(athletics) "Steeplechase (athletics)") while finishing second to Nurmi in the 5,000 m and cross country. [Albin Stenroos](/wiki/Albin_Stenroos "Albin Stenroos") won the [marathon](/wiki/Marathon_(sport) "Marathon (sport)"), while the [Finnish](/wiki/Finland "Finland") team (with Nurmi and Ritola) was victorious in the 3,000 m and cross-country team events.
* The British runners [Harold Abrahams](/wiki/Harold_Abrahams "Harold Abrahams") and [Eric Liddell](/wiki/Eric_Liddell "Eric Liddell") won the 100 m and the 400 m events, respectively. Liddell refused to compete in the 100-metre sprint because it was held on a Sunday, and he was an observant Christian. Their stories were depicted in the 1981 film *[Chariots of Fire](/wiki/Chariots_of_Fire "Chariots of Fire")*. In addition, [Douglas Lowe](/wiki/Douglas_Lowe_(athlete) "Douglas Lowe (athlete)") won the 800-metre competition.
* [DeHart Hubbard](/wiki/DeHart_Hubbard "DeHart Hubbard") became the first African-American to win an individual gold medal in the [Long jump](/wiki/Long_jump "Long jump").
* The [marathon](/wiki/Marathon_(sport) "Marathon (sport)") distance was fixed at 42.195 km (26.219 mi), from the distance run at the [1908 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1908_Summer_Olympics "1908 Summer Olympics") in London.
* The 1924 Olympics were the first to use the standard 50 m pool with marked lanes.
* Dual-sport athlete [Johnny Weissmuller](/wiki/Johnny_Weissmuller "Johnny Weissmuller") won three gold medals in swimming and one bronze in [water polo](/wiki/Water_polo "Water polo").
* [Harold Osborn](/wiki/Harold_Osborn "Harold Osborn") won gold medals and set Olympic records in the high jump and the 1924 Olympic decathlon. His 6' 6" high jump remained the Olympic record for 12 years, while his decathlon score of 7,710.775 points also set a world record and resulted in worldwide press coverage recognizing him as the "world's greatest athlete."
* [Fencer](/wiki/Fencing_(sport) "Fencing (sport)") [Roger Ducret](/wiki/Roger_Ducret "Roger Ducret") of France won five medals, of which three were gold.
* In gymnastics, 24 men scored a perfect 10. Twenty-three scored it in the now-discontinued [rope climbing](/wiki/Rope_climbing "Rope climbing") event. Albert Seguin scored a ten here and a perfect ten on the side vault.
* The Olympic motto *[Citius, Altius, Fortius](/wiki/Olympic_symbols "Olympic symbols")* (Faster, Higher, Stronger) was used for the first time at the Olympics. It had been used before by the [Union des Sociétés Françaises de Sports Athlétiques](/wiki/Union_des_Soci%C3%A9t%C3%A9s_Fran%C3%A7aises_de_Sports_Athl%C3%A9tiques "Union des Sociétés Françaises de Sports Athlétiques"), a French sporting federation whose founding members included [Pierre de Coubertin](/wiki/Pierre_de_Coubertin "Pierre de Coubertin").[[5]](#cite_note-5) De Coubertin took the motto from his friend [Henri Didon](/wiki/Henri_Didon "Henri Didon"), a [Dominican priest](/wiki/Dominican_Order "Dominican Order") who had coined the phrase during a speech before a Paris youth gathering of 1891.[[6]](#cite_note-motto-6)
* [Ireland](/wiki/Ireland_at_the_Olympics "Ireland at the Olympics") was given formal recognition as an independent nation in the Olympic Movement in Paris in 1924, and it was at these games that Ireland made its first appearance in the Olympic Games as an independent nation.
* Originally called *Semaine des Sports d'Hiver* ("Week of Winter Sports") and held in association with the 1924 Summer Olympics, the sports competitions held in [Chamonix](/wiki/Chamonix "Chamonix") between 25 January and 5 February 1924 were later designated by the International Olympic Committee (IOC) as the I Olympic Winter Games. ([1924 Winter Olympics](/wiki/1924_Winter_Olympics "1924 Winter Olympics"))
* These were the first Games to have an [Olympic Village](/wiki/Olympic_Village "Olympic Village").
* The [Art competitions at the 1924 Summer Olympics](/wiki/Art_competitions_at_the_1924_Summer_Olympics "Art competitions at the 1924 Summer Olympics") were the first time that the [Olympic Art competitions](/wiki/Art_competitions_at_the_Summer_Olympics "Art competitions at the Summer Olympics") were contested seriously, with 193 entries in five categories. A total of 14 medals were awarded, though none were given in the music category.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1924_Summer_Olympics&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Jeux de L’Enfance")]

The Jeux de L’Enfance, a program of youth sports competitions and activities, were held by Olympic organizers alongside the Games in cooperation with the [YMCA](/wiki/YMCA "YMCA").[[9]](#cite_note-Barker_2015-9)[[10]](#cite_note-Report_628-10) The following future Olympic sports were exhibited:

## Participating nations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1924_Summer_Olympics&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Participating nations")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/08/1924_Summer_Olympic_games_countries.png/250px-1924_Summer_Olympic_games_countries.png)](/wiki/File:1924_Summer_Olympic_games_countries.png)

Participating Countries of the 1924 Olympiad

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/63/1924_Summer_olympics_team_numbers.png/250px-1924_Summer_olympics_team_numbers.png)](/wiki/File:1924_Summer_olympics_team_numbers.png)

Number of athletes

A total of 44 nations were represented at the 1924 Games. [Germany](/wiki/Germany_at_the_Olympics "Germany at the Olympics") was still absent, having not been invited by the Organizing Committee.[[28]](#cite_note-GuttmannGermany-28) [China](/wiki/Republic_of_China_at_the_Olympics "Republic of China at the Olympics") (although it did not compete), [Ecuador](/wiki/Ecuador_at_the_Olympics "Ecuador at the Olympics"), [Ireland](/wiki/Ireland_at_the_Olympics "Ireland at the Olympics"), [Lithuania](/wiki/Lithuania_at_the_Olympics "Lithuania at the Olympics"), and [Uruguay](/wiki/Uruguay_at_the_Olympics "Uruguay at the Olympics") attended the Olympic Games for the first time, while the [Philippines](/wiki/Philippines_at_the_Olympics "Philippines at the Olympics") competed for first time in an Olympic Games as a nation (though it first participated in the 1900 Summer Olympic Games). [Latvia](/wiki/Latvia_at_the_Olympics "Latvia at the Olympics") and [Poland](/wiki/Poland_at_the_Olympics "Poland at the Olympics") also attended the Summer Olympic Games for the first time (having both appeared earlier at the [1924 Winter Olympics](/wiki/1924_Winter_Olympics "1924 Winter Olympics") in [Chamonix](/wiki/Chamonix "Chamonix")).

The 1924 Olympics saw a return of the following nations: [Austria](/wiki/Austria "Austria"), [Bulgaria](/wiki/Bulgaria "Bulgaria"), [Cuba](/wiki/Cuba "Cuba"), [Haiti](/wiki/Haiti "Haiti"), [Hungary](/wiki/Hungary "Hungary"), [Mexico](/wiki/Mexico "Mexico"), [Romania](/wiki/Romania "Romania") and [Turkey](/wiki/Turkey "Turkey").

At the time, [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia"), [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand "New Zealand"), [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada"), [South Africa](/wiki/South_Africa "South Africa") and [Ireland](/wiki/Irish_Free_State "Irish Free State") were all dominions of the [British Empire](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire"). [India](/wiki/India "India") was also part of British Empire, but was not a dominion. For other sovereign states (i.e. United States, France, Brazil, Japan, etc) and the international community as a whole (i.e [League of Nations](/wiki/League_of_Nations "League of Nations")) the term [dominion](/wiki/British_Dominion "British Dominion"), used internally in the British Empire, was very ambiguous, meaning "something between a colony and state". It was only years later with the [Statute of Westminster 1931](/wiki/Statute_of_Westminster_1931 "Statute of Westminster 1931") that this ambiguity would be dispelled.

And [Philippines](/wiki/Philippines "Philippines") was an [unincorporated territory](/wiki/Territory_of_the_United_States "Territory of the United States") and [commonwealth](/wiki/Commonwealth_(U.S._insular_area) "Commonwealth (U.S. insular area)") of the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States").

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1924_Summer_Olympics_medal_table&action=edit&section=T-1 "Edit section: Art competitions")]

Note: [Art competitions at the 1924 Summer Olympics#Medal\_table](/wiki/Art_competitions_at_the_1924_Summer_Olympics#Medal_table "Art competitions at the 1924 Summer Olympics")

[![A man in swimming attire holding an emblem inscribed with "Olympic Games" in Greek lettering and the numbers 776 and 1896](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/18/Hajos.jpg/250px-Hajos.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hajos.jpg)

Alfréd Hajós at the [1896 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1896_Summer_Olympics "1896 Summer Olympics"), where he became the first Olympic champion in swimming before winning the silver medal at these Games

The 1924 Summer Olympics also included [art competitions](/wiki/Art_competitions_at_the_1924_Summer_Olympics "Art competitions at the 1924 Summer Olympics") across five disciplines: architecture, literature, music, painting, and sculpture, for works inspired by sport-related themes, were medal-eligible events at the time.[[31]](#cite_note-museum-32)[[32]](#cite_note-music-33) [Art competitions](/wiki/Art_competitions_at_the_Summer_Olympics "Art competitions at the Summer Olympics") were held from the [1912 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1912_Summer_Olympics "1912 Summer Olympics") until the [1948 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1948_Summer_Olympics "1948 Summer Olympics"), but were discontinued over the [amateurism controversy](/wiki/Olympic_Games#Amateurism_and_professionalism "Olympic Games").[[33]](#cite_note-34) Medals in art competitions are no longer recognized by the IOC as part of the total medal count.[[34]](#cite_note-smith-35)[[35]](#cite_note-36)

In architecture, no gold medal was awarded while the silver was awarded to the pair of [Alfréd Hajós](/wiki/Alfr%C3%A9d_Haj%C3%B3s "Alfréd Hajós") and [Dezső Lauber](/wiki/Dezs%C5%91_Lauber "Dezső Lauber") of [Hungary](/wiki/Hungary_at_the_1924_Summer_Olympics "Hungary at the 1924 Summer Olympics"). Hajós became one of only two Olympians ever to have won medals in both sport (two golds in [swimming at the 1896 Summer Olympics](/wiki/Swimming_at_the_1896_Summer_Olympics "Swimming at the 1896 Summer Olympics")) and art Olympic competitions.[[36]](#cite_note-37)[[37]](#cite_note-38) The bronze was awarded to [Julien Médecin](/wiki/Julien_M%C3%A9decin "Julien Médecin") of [Monaco](/wiki/Monaco_at_the_1924_Summer_Olympics "Monaco at the 1924 Summer Olympics"), who became the first Monégasque competitor to win an Olympic medal.[[b]](#cite_note-fn1-39)[[38]](#cite_note-40)

In literature, one gold, which was awarded to [Géo-Charles](/wiki/G%C3%A9o-Charles "Géo-Charles") of France, two silvers to [Josef Petersen](/wiki/Josef_Petersen "Josef Petersen") of [Denmark](/wiki/Denmark "Denmark") and [Margaret Stuart](/wiki/Margaret_Stuart_(poet) "Margaret Stuart (poet)") of Great Britain, and two bronzes to [Charles Gonnet](/wiki/Charles_Gonnet "Charles Gonnet") of France and [Oliver Gogarty](/wiki/Oliver_Gogarty "Oliver Gogarty") of [Ireland](/wiki/Ireland_at_the_1924_Summer_Olympics "Ireland at the 1924 Summer Olympics"), were awarded.[[31]](#cite_note-museum-32) Two compositions, entitled "The Land Where the Rose is Grown" and "O Vigila (now let the games begin)", that were part of the literature competition, were also part of the music competitions. The compositions were made by [George Bamber](/wiki/George_Bamber "George Bamber") of Great Britain, though no medals were awarded in the music competition as the jury could not reach a consensus to award medals for any of the competitors.[[32]](#cite_note-music-33)

In painting, [Jean Jacoby](/wiki/Jean_Jacoby "Jean Jacoby") of [Luxembourg](/wiki/Luxembourg_at_the_1924_Summer_Olympics "Luxembourg at the 1924 Summer Olympics") won the gold medal, becoming the first Luxembourgish competitor to win an Olympic gold medal,[[c]](#cite_note-44) [Jack Butler Yeats](/wiki/Jack_Butler_Yeats "Jack Butler Yeats") of Ireland won the silver, becoming the first Irish competitor to win an Olympic medal,[[b]](#cite_note-fn1-39)[[42]](#cite_note-45) and [Johan van Hell](/wiki/Johan_van_Hell "Johan van Hell") of the [Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands_at_the_1924_Summer_Olympics "Netherlands at the 1924 Summer Olympics") won the bronze.[[43]](#cite_note-46)

In sculpture, [Konstantinos Dimitriadis](/wiki/Konstantinos_Dimitriadis "Konstantinos Dimitriadis") of [Greece](/wiki/Greece_at_the_1924_Summer_Olympics "Greece at the 1924 Summer Olympics") won the gold medal, [Frantz Heldenstein](/wiki/Frantz_Heldenstein "Frantz Heldenstein") of Luxembourg won the silver, and two bronze medals were awarded, one to [Jean René Gauguin](/wiki/Jean_Ren%C3%A9_Gauguin "Jean René Gauguin") of Denmark and the other to [Claude-Léon Mascaux](/wiki/Claude-L%C3%A9on_Mascaux "Claude-Léon Mascaux") of France.[[44]](#cite_note-47)

## Legacy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1924_Summer_Olympics&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Legacy")]

The 1924 Summer Olympics was the second edition of the Summer Olympics to be held in Paris. 100 years later, the city has hosted the games once again with the [2024 Summer Olympics](/wiki/2024_Summer_Olympics "2024 Summer Olympics"), marking its third time, becoming the second city ever to host the [Summer Olympics](/wiki/Summer_Olympic_Games "Summer Olympic Games") three times (after [London](/wiki/London "London"), which hosted the [1908](/wiki/1908_Summer_Olympics "1908 Summer Olympics"), [1948](/wiki/1948_Summer_Olympics "1948 Summer Olympics"), and [2012](/wiki/2012_Summer_Olympics "2012 Summer Olympics") Games). Paris 2024 also marks the [centenary](/wiki/Centenary "Centenary") of [Chamonix 1924](/wiki/1924_Winter_Olympics "1924 Winter Olympics"), which in turn marks the [centenary](/wiki/1924_Winter_Olympics "1924 Winter Olympics") of the [Winter Olympics](/wiki/Winter_Olympic_Games "Winter Olympic Games"); making Paris 2024 the sixth Olympic Games hosted by France (three Summer Olympics and three [Winter Olympics](/wiki/Winter_Olympic_Games "Winter Olympic Games")), and the first French Olympics since the [1992 Winter Games](/wiki/1992_Winter_Olympics "1992 Winter Olympics") in [Albertville](/wiki/Albertville "Albertville").

Notable debuts of participating countries for the Paris 1924 Olympics include [Ireland](/wiki/Ireland_at_the_Olympics "Ireland at the Olympics"), [Latvia](/wiki/Latvia_at_the_Olympics "Latvia at the Olympics"), [Lithuania](/wiki/Lithuania_at_the_Olympics "Lithuania at the Olympics"), [Philippines](/wiki/Philippines_at_the_Olympics "Philippines at the Olympics"), [Poland](/wiki/Poland_at_the_Olympics "Poland at the Olympics"), [Romania](/wiki/Romania_at_the_Olympics "Romania at the Olympics"), and [Uruguay](/wiki/Uruguay_at_the_Olympics "Uruguay at the Olympics"); all of which celebrated their centenary participation at the 2024 Summer Olympics back in Paris.

One venue from the 1924 Games was used in the 2024 Games. The extensively renovated and downsized main stadium, known since 1928 as [Stade Olympique Yves-du-Manoir](/wiki/Stade_Olympique_Yves-du-Manoir "Stade Olympique Yves-du-Manoir"), hosted field hockey.

The last surviving competitor of the 1924 Summer Olympics was [Croatian](/wiki/Croatia "Croatia") swimmer [Ivo Pavelić](/wiki/Ivo_Paveli%C4%87 "Ivo Pavelić"), who died on 22 February 2011 at the age of 103; he competed for Yugoslavia, which Croatia was part of at the time.[[45]](#cite_note-48)

Continuation of Jeux de L’Enfance, games for youth sports and competition, from the Paris 1924 was embodied through the creation of [Youth Olympics Games](/wiki/Youth_Olympic_Games "Youth Olympic Games") inaugurating in [Singapore](/wiki/Singapore "Singapore") with the [2010 Summer Youth Olympics](/wiki/2010_Summer_Youth_Olympics "2010 Summer Youth Olympics") with 3,600 athletes aged 14–18 from 204 nations competing in 201 events in 26 sports.
